lessons.title: std::flat_map and std::flat_set: Array-Backed Associative Containers
lessons.motivation: std::map and std::set are C++ workhorses, but under the hood they use a red-black tree: every node is a separate heap allocation, and traversing to the next element means chasing a pointer. On read-heavy workloads — lookups and iteration — this causes constant cache misses. C++23 introduced std::flat_map and std::flat_set: containers with the same interface but storing data in sorted vectors. Binary search over a vector is far more cache-friendly than a tree, and for the typical use case of "fill once, query many times" this delivers a measurable speedup without changing calling code.
lessons.theory:
std::flat_map<K, V> stores keys and values in two separate sorted vectors. Lookup is performed by binary search over the keys vector in O(log n), just like std::map, but accessing consecutive keys walks contiguous memory — the CPU cache is used efficiently. Header: <flat_map>. std::flat_set<T> similarly stores elements in a single sorted vector; header: <flat_set>.

The interface of flat_map and flat_set deliberately mirrors map/set: operator[], at(), find(), contains(), insert(), erase(), and range-for over key-value pairs. Switching from map to flat_map often amounts to changing one type name. One important difference concerns iterators: dereferencing a flat_map iterator returns a proxy pair of references (std::pair<const K&, V&>), which is a temporary object — structured bindings must be declared as auto [k, v], not auto& [k, v].

Performance depends on the access pattern. flat_map wins for reads, iteration, and small-to-medium datasets (thousands to tens of thousands of elements). It loses when elements are inserted frequently in the middle: each insertion requires shifting elements in O(n). If the input data is already sorted, the std::sorted_unique tag lets you construct the container without an extra sort pass. Decision rule: build the table once and only read it afterward — prefer flat_map; insert and erase frequently at arbitrary positions — use regular map.

flat_map exposes its underlying storage through keys() and values(), which return const references to the internal vectors. This allows passing the key list to an algorithm without copying, enables SIMD-friendly sequential access, or simply retrieves the size without iterating. The default backing container is std::vector, but any random-access container (e.g. std::deque) can be substituted via template parameters.

examples.title: Basic flat_map: same interface as map
examples.expectedOutput:
Alice: 98
Bob: 87
Carol: 92
contains Dave: false
examples.explanation: flat_map behaves exactly like map: the duplicate assignment scores["Alice"] = 98 updates the value, iteration visits elements in sorted key order, and contains() uses binary search. Notice auto [name, score] without &: a flat_map iterator returns a proxy pair of temporary references, and binding by lvalue reference does not compile.

examples.title: flat_set and the sorted_unique tag
examples.explanation: flat_set keeps elements in a sorted vector and silently drops duplicates on insert. The std::sorted_unique tag signals to the flat_map constructor that the input is already ordered and unique — the container skips the O(n log n) sort and copies the data directly. Only use this tag when you can guarantee order and uniqueness: violating either is undefined behavior.

examples.title: Configuration table: find() and keys()
examples.expectedOutput:
port = 8080
retries = 3
missing not found
key count: 5
examples.explanation: flat_map is an excellent fit for configuration tables: built once at startup, then used exclusively for reads. find() performs binary search and returns an iterator or end(). keys() gives direct access to the internal keys vector without copying — handy for passing to algorithms or logging. Similarly, values() exposes the values vector.
